s2.q: What is the reported incidence of shoulder dystocia?
s2.explain: Shoulder dystocia occurs in 0.2-0.3% of deliveries.

s2.q: Which two adjectives best describe shoulder dystocia?
s2.explain: Shoulder dystocia is unpredictable (cannot be reliably forecast) and unpreventable (no prophylactic intervention).

options: Arrested delivery of fetal shoulder after head delivery requiring additional maneuvers
Failure of fetal rotation in pelvic outlet
s2.explain: It is specifically the arrested delivery of the fetal shoulder after delivery of the fetal head, requiring additional maneuvers.

options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance

s5.q: Which is an antepartum risk factor for shoulder dystocia?
s5.explain: Previous shoulder dystocia (recurrence ~10%), macrosomia, DM, BMI > 30, and induction of labor are the 5 antepartum risk factors.

s5.q: Which is an intrapartum risk factor for shoulder dystocia?
s5.explain: Prolonged 1st/2nd stage, secondary arrest, oxytocin augmentation, and assisted vaginal delivery are the 5 intrapartum risk factors.

s5.q: The single most common risk factor for shoulder dystocia is:
s5.explain: Macrosomia (BW > 4,000 g or > 4,500 g in diabetic mothers) is the single most common risk factor.

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s5.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s6.q: Which maneuver is the most effective single intervention in shoulder dystocia?
s6.explain: McRobert's maneuver (hyperflexion of maternal hips) is the most effective intervention, resolving ~90% when combined with suprapubic pressure.

s6.q: McRobert's + suprapubic pressure resolves approximately what % of cases?
s6.explain: The combination resolves approximately 90% of shoulder dystocia cases.

s6.q: What is the purpose of episiotomy in shoulder dystocia management?
options: To facilitate internal maneuvers by enlarging the vaginal opening
s6.explain: Episiotomy alone does not resolve dystocia — it enlarges the opening to allow room for internal manipulation.

s6.q: Which complication is most associated with delivery of the posterior arm?
s6.explain: Delivery of the posterior arm carries a higher risk of fracturing the humerus or clavicle, which usually heal well.

s6.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s6.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s8.q: Which fetal complication involves injury to C5-C6 nerve roots causing 'waiter's tip' deformity?
s8.explain: Erb's palsy affects C5-C6 roots, causing 'waiter's tip' deformity (arm adducted, internally rotated, forearm pronated).

s8.q: Which is a maternal complication of shoulder dystocia?
s8.explain: PPH (from uterine atony or perineal trauma) is one of 4 maternal complications: PPH, lacerations/tears, uterine rupture, psychological stress.

s8.q: How many fetal vs maternal complications are listed for shoulder dystocia?
s8.explain: 5 fetal (brachial plexus injury, Erb's palsy, fractures, hypoxic brain injury, death) and 4 maternal.

options: All of the above are possible
s8.explain: Obstetric conditions can lead to hemorrhage, infection, thromboembolism, and other complications.
Fetal well-being is closely linked to maternal condition — multiple fetal complications are possible.
